description: A vertical (portrait) designed graphic combining a stock photo with a dark navy text panel. The top two-thirds is a warm-toned stock photograph of an immigration-interview-like scene: a bearded man wearing glasses and a gray blazer over a white shirt sits at a light wooden table gesturing with both hands as he speaks, facing a second person seen from behind (dark-haired, in a dark jacket) who is holding a notepad and pen, suggesting an applicant being interviewed by an officer. Bookshelves are blurred in the background. The bottom third is a solid dark navy blue panel containing, centered, the circular U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal (eagle), the agency wordmark 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' in gray serif type, then a bold headline in golden-yellow sans-serif reading 'Tell the truth in your interview' followed by white text 'If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life.' The visual style is clean, corporate, government-PSA: muted photo, official seal, two-tone typography (yellow emphasis line over white). The messaging technique is a deterrence/fear appeal warning immigration applicants that lying during USCIS interviews carries a permanent (lifetime) bar from the United States; the tweet ties it to a specific case of an alien convicted for concealing a criminal past. Target audience: immigration benefit applicants and the public, signaling strict vetting and 'make America safe' enforcement posture.
notable_text: Tell the truth in your interview / If you lie, you could be banned from the U.S. for life. / U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services
